articles.title: Luxury meat wholesaler PFD opens Port Washington shop
articles.author: Erica Marcus
articles.article_text: --- title: Luxury meat wholesaler PFD opens Port Washington shop author: Erica Marcus url: https://www.newsday.com/lifestyle/restaurants/pfd-prime-food-distributors-port-washington-qodfloue hostname: newsday.com description: The hidden shop deals in steaks and chops usually only found in high-end restaurants. sitename: Newsday date: 2026-04-04 categories: ['Things to Do'] tags: ['Restaurants, FeedMe'] --- "If you know, you know" could well be the motto of Prime Food Distributor. The 100-employee high-end meat supplier has no sales reps: Principals John Kosmidis and Nick Castellana rely entirely on the word of mouth generated by their chef customers — many of whom you know by name. They are bringing that IYKYK energy to a tiny 3-month-old "retail meat boutique," carved out of a corner of their headquarters that is tucked away in an office park in Port Washington. The only possible foot traffic is from employees who can’t find a parking spot closer to the front door. "This is a passion project," Castellana said. "We wanted people to see what we do." Added Kosmidis, "We were tired of telling friends and family who didn’t want to shop in the supermarket that they couldn’t buy from us." But, of course, the two also have a bigger business plan. "This store is ‘proof of concept’ for something we want to expand," he continued. "And the location is perfect because it operates under a microscope that we can keep an eye on." All the meat here is precut and vacuum-packed. The first display that customers see is what Kosmidis and Castellana call their Monday-to-Friday selection, items that they themselves cook on weeknights. Here you’ll find flank, skirt and strip steaks as well as less common cuts, such as bavette and minute steaks cut from the loin. Prices range from $12.95 to $29.95 and the grades range from choice to prime, with much of it from the top end of choice — that is, having more intramuscular marbling than it needs to qualify for that grade, but not quite enough to achieve prime status. PFD creates burgers for scores of high-end restaurants and, here, they sell two blends, one prime ("meritage," $14.95) and one American Wagyu ("lux," $19.95). Over in the premium case, you’ll find such steaks-of-the-moment as tomahawks, top sirloin caps (AKA culotte or picanha), rib-eye caps (spinalis dorsi) and bone-in tenderloins and filet mignons in various combinations of top choice, prime, American Wagyu and dry-aged. The house style for dry aging emphasizes concentration of flavor over the development of cheesy funk. The shop carries much more than just beef. There are Giannone chickens from Canada, plump birds that resemble the famous French poulets de Bresse; pork and lamb chops; packages of veal osso bucco where every piece has a big, fat hole filled with marrow. Alongside the meticulously "Frenched" racks of lamb I was gratified to see untrimmed racks — Castellana agreed that "the finger meat" attached to the bone is some of the best eating a sheep can provide. You’ll also find a nice assortment of oils, vinegars, salts and seasonings. And you can have a free espresso. Plus, all orders are packed into insulated bags with an ice pack. PFD's 25,000-square-foot headquarters also houses the company’s state-of-the-art fulfillment center, whence meat is loaded onto trucks that are sent as far south as Miami, as far north as Boston and as far west as Chicago. Still other trucks are headed to freighters and airplanes to dispatch products to 20 international destinations. The actual cutting and fabrication are done in a nearby building (also 25,000 square feet) where Kosmidis, Castellana and Castellana’s father, Joe, started the business in 2012. The Castellana family had been involved in Western Beef, a discount retail chain, and Kosmidis started working as a butcher in Astoria, Queens, when he was 11. All three men knew the meat business backwards and forwards. "There is no shortage of commodity meat purveyors," Kosmidis noted. "Our goal was to create a company that focuses on producing the best steaks on the planet." PFD, 4 Tri Harbor Ct., Port Washington, 516-723-2200, hprimefooddistributor.com. Open Monday to Friday 9 a.m. to 6 p.m., Saturday 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.

articles.title: Salt on the Water taking over former ITA Kitchen in East Moriches
articles.author: Erica Marcus
articles.article_text: --- title: Salt on the Water taking over former ITA Kitchen in East Moriches author: Erica Marcus url: https://www.newsday.com/lifestyle/restaurants/ita-kitchen-east-moriches-salt-merrick-kmm7x90k hostname: newsday.com description: The menu will be same as the Merrick original. sitename: Newsday date: 2026-04-01 categories: ['Things to Do'] tags: ['Restaurants, FeedMe'] --- For three summers, ITA Kitchen has offered magnificent views of Tutthill Cove and Moriches Bay from its perch in East Moriches’ Atlantic Marina, but now the Italian American eatery’s signature faux-flower archway has been dismantled to make way for a second location of Merrick’s Salt on the Water, a top waterfront restaurant. The two businesses have overlapping ownership: Christina and Salvatore Sorrentino operate ITA Kitchens in Garden City South and Patchogue. (The original, opened in Bay Shore in 2019, closed last year.) Salt’s co-owner, John Cestare, is also a partner at ITA Kitchen and has had an eye on the property for years. "We always thought of this as a pop-up," Christina said. "We were holding it until it became a Salt." Salt, it would seem, is a fitting concept for the spot. Since 2016, its sprawling deck has attracted crowds to the shores of Merrick’s East Bay, a body of water overlooked by only one other eatery, Fabio’s. Cestare, whose partners in Salt are Matt McBride and Steve Buccella, said 2023 would have been premature to open in East Moriches. "This wasn’t something that could happen overnight, and we weren’t ready to do it at that point." Renovations have begun and, he said, the menu will be identical to the one in Merrick. He is shooting for a June opening.

articles.title: Josie's Pizza Bar shifts from truck to Glen Cove shop
articles.author: Erica Marcus
articles.article_text: --- title: Josie's Pizza Bar shifts from truck to Glen Cove shop author: Erica Marcus url: https://www.newsday.com/lifestyle/restaurants/josies-pizza-bar-glen-cove-cx2ux41s hostname: newsday.com description: It's now an all-weather destination — with beer and wine too. sitename: Newsday date: 2026-04-02 categories: ['Things to Do'] tags: ['Restaurants, FeedMe, FMOpen'] --- Josephine Giglio doesn’t do anything the easy way and, after years of being a mobile pizzaiola, she was reluctant to move her operation inside. She’d parked her Josie's Pizza Bar truck across from the water in Glen Cove’s Garvies Point since 2022 and, she said, "a store didn’t excite me. I didn’t want to be like everyone else." Nevertheless, she made her last pizzas on the truck last August and, two months later, opened in the adjacent storefront that had been Heritage Bakers, the popover specialist that closed in 2021. "We just outgrew the truck," she said, "and our customers were ready for the next thing." Giglio, who has been slinging pies since she was in high school, had been using part of the Heritage space for prep and storage; now she created a light-filled, 20-seat dining area in the front and, facing the wood-burning oven, a proper pizza bar where six customers can sit and watch her and her crew create pizzas, panini, salads and, for the first time, a daily fresh pasta such as pappardelle Bolognese. They can even enjoy beer and wine. The five picnic tables from the truck set-up are still outside, making Josie’s the rare al fresco pizza destination on Long Island. After sweltering during the summer and freezing in the winter, even Giglio has to admit that being indoors has its advantages: "The weather just doesn’t matter to us anymore — and it is nice not having to lug everything to the truck." All the truck pizzas have stayed on the menu, from the simple Marg-A-Rita (with Italian tomatoes and fresh mozzarella from Brooklyn’s Lioni Latticini), the Boozy (with vodka sauce) and the Little Piggy (with tiny Battistoni "cup and char" pepperoni and lots of basil) to the more fanciful Honey Bomb (with Calabrian chili spread, hot soppressata and honey) and the frequent special, Gracefully Shroomed, a white pie with mushrooms and white truffle oil. The pies themselves ($18 to $25) exhibit a blend of tradition and idiosyncrasy. They are cooked in a Neapolitan-style wood-burning oven but, instead of an airy-holey rim and a droopy middle, these pies are puffy throughout, with the fine-grained texture that comes from not fermenting the dough for days. Almost all ingredients are imported — from the Sicilian oregano branches that she shakes and sieves to the Sicilian sea salt, fine for the dough, coarse for sprinkling on salads. Giglio recently made the switch from Sicilian Partanna olive oil to Corto, grown and pressed in California’s Central Valley. "I couldn’t deny I liked it better," she conceded. "And, like I said, I like to go my own way." Josie’s Pizza Bar, 1 Garvies Point Rd., Glen Cove, 646-918-4944, josiespizzabar.com. Open Wednesday to Saturday noon to 10 p.m., Sunday noon to 8 p.m.

articles.title: Moneyline closes in Long Beach, Mareya Mexican coming
articles.author: Melissa Azofeifa
articles.article_text: --- title: Moneyline closes in Long Beach, Mareya Mexican coming author: Melissa Azofeifa url: https://www.newsday.com/lifestyle/restaurants/moneyline-mareya-long-beach-xeicbakv hostname: newsday.com description: Long Beach is getting a new taco spot before summertime. sitename: Newsday date: 2026-04-01 categories: ['Things to Do'] tags: ['Restaurants, FeedMe, FMClose'] --- Tacos, cocktails and a fresh vibe are heading to Long Beach as Mareya, a new Mexican restaurant, takes over the former Moneyline space. This is owner Mike Wachs' first restaurant and he hopes to open by Cinco de Mayo or May 5. "I have a big responsibility to bring something very special here," said Wachs, a former hospitality consultant. Mareya’s name was inspired by the Spanish word "marea," which translates to "tide." Wachs was inspired by the bay and ocean that surround the barrier island, taking some artistic liberty with the spelling. Moneyline opened at 906 W. Beech St. last year with a concept modeled after Las Vegas sportsbooks, Newsday previously reported, serving dishes like seared ahi tuna, Cobb salad and "grown up mac & cheese." In hindsight, owner Josh Krapf acknowledged that "maybe it wasn’t the best fit for Long Beach" in a statement about the closing. Now he and his wife are following the progress of the venue's transformation and are "hopeful the town will embrace the new concept which will be much more beach- and family friendly." While Mareya’s menu is still a work in progress, Wachs is locked into tacos and desserts. He also wants community input to help create the menu. "Right now, we’re cooking everything and we’re seeing what goes, what fits," Wachs said. "We want to launch with a smaller menu; it’s going to grow and it’s going to evolve, but it’s so important to come out the gate — whatever you do it needs to be great." Wachs took inspiration from Mexico City "with a sprinkle of Tulum," to create an immersive experience in the decor, he said. The space that once was accented with neon pink and laden with more than 20 television screens, will be transformed into an oasis with greenery, earth tones, terra-cotta and pops of color.

articles.title: Cops Hunt Gunman; Bragg Returns Antiquities to Italy & Vatican
articles.author: Brian Berger
articles.article_text: --- title: Cops Hunt Gunman; Bragg Returns Antiquities to Italy & Vatican author: Brian Berger url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/crime-watch/cops-hunt-gunman-bragg-returns-antiquities-to-italy-vatican-BD5731993 hostname: ourtownny.com description: An unidentified individual discharged a firearm multiple times on March 11 at approximately 11 p.m. in front of 435 East 102nd Street. The suspect then fled on ... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-06 tags: ['alvin bragg', 'brian berger', 'gunman', 'antiquities'] --- Cops Hunt Gunman; Bragg Returns Antiquities to Italy & Vatican An unidentified individual discharged a firearm multiple times on March 11 at approximately 11 p.m. in front of 435 East 102nd Street. The suspect then fled on foot. Cops are asking for the publicâs assistance in identifying the gunman sought in connection with the reckless endangerment incident within the confines of the 23rd Precinct. The suspect is described as a Black or Hispanic male with a dark beard and mustache wearing an orange puffer jacket and torn blue jeans. This address is within the 85-year-old NYCHA East River Houses complex, which is home to around 2000 people today. Anyone with information regarding this incident is asked to call the NYPDâs Crime Stoppers Hotline at 1-800-577-TIPS (8477) or for Spanish, 1-888-57-PISTA (74782). The public can also submit their tips by logging onto the Crime Stoppers website at https://crimestoppers.nypdonline.org/, or on X @NYPDTips. Bragg Returns Pilfered Antiquities to Italy and The Vatican Seventeen antiquities and rare books, collectively valued at more than $1.5 million, were returned to to Italy and the Vatican by Manhattan district attorney Alvin Bragg. The objects were recovered pursuant to multiple investigations into antiquities trafficking networks and had been previously allegedly removed from Italy trafficked by Frieda Tchakos as well as by several convicted antiquitiesâ smugglers, including Giovanni Franco Becchina and Fritz and Harry Burki, among others. âI am grateful to the Italian authorities for their ongoing collaboration and partnership, which continues to yield significant results in the fight to undo the harm caused by trafficking networks,â Bragg said in a statement. âCultural heritage represents a nationâs deepest identity, its legacy of values embedded along the course of the centuries,â said Italian Minister of Culture Alessandro Giuli. The Consulate General of Italy is at 690 Park Avenue at East 69th Street. Among the pieces returned: Six Chinese Jesuit Books, dating to the 16th-17th centuries, were stolen from the Archives of the Society of Jesus in Vatican City. The books were last documented in Archives in the early 1970s by Jesuit scholar Albert Chan and next appeared in 2000 when they were offered for sale on the antiquarian book market in London. After being purchased by a private collector, the books were loaned to the Department of Rare Books and Special Collections at the University of Notre Dame, where they were ultimately seized and voluntarily surrendered to the Office in late 2025. Letter by Alfonso I dâEste, Duke of Ferrara, to Lodovico Ariosto (Governor of the Province of Garfagnana), dated January 31, 1524. The letter was stolen from the State Archive of Massa, Italy, smuggled into the U.S., and eventually sold at Sothebyâs NY in 1973 to the Morgan Library & Museum. The Letter remained in the Morganâs collection until it was seized and voluntarily surrendered to the Office earlier this year.

articles.title: Mamdani Campaigned on Fulfilling NYCâs Class Size Mandate
articles.author: Alex Zimmerman; Chalkbeat
articles.article_text: --- title: Mamdani Campaigned on Fulfilling NYCâs Class Size Mandate author: Alex Zimmerman; Chalkbeat url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/mamdani-campaigned-on-fulfilling-nyc-s-class-size-mandate-KK5729815 hostname: ourtownny.com description: This story was originally published by Chalkbeat. Sign up for their newsletters at ckbe.at/newsletters Mamdani campaigned on fulfilling NYCâs class size mandate... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-03 tags: ['education', 'mayor zohran mamdani', 'alex zimmerman', 'chalkbeat'] --- Mamdani Campaigned on Fulfilling NYCâs Class Size Mandate So why is he pushing for a delay? This story was originally published by Chalkbeat. Sign up for their newsletters at ckbe.at/newsletters Mamdani campaigned on fulfilling NYCâs class size mandate. So why is he pushing for a delay? On the campaign trail, Mayor Zohran Mamdani vowed to take a state mandate to slash class sizes seriously, saying it âwill transform our studentsâ ability to learn.â âThe question of compliance has too often been a negotiation,â Mamdani said on the first day of school in September. Now, the Mamdani administration is seeking to do just that: negotiate with lawmakers in Albany to give the city more time to comply. Some of the class size lawâs key backers, including its legislative sponsor, seem willing to play ball. State Sen. John Liu, who sponsored the class size law, said he is open to allowing the city to reach full compliance over the next four years instead of the legally required two. A deal to extend the class size law may come as part of the state budget â and lawmakers could use it as leverage as they simultaneously debate whether to extend Mamdaniâs control of the school system, which expires at the end of June. (The state budget, which was due Wednesday, is delayed, and itâs unclear exactly when it will be finalized.) On the campaign trail, Mamdani said it was âcriticalâ to deliver smaller classes across the system. Hereâs why as mayor heâs now pushing for an extension on the class size law. Next yearâs class size reduction target may be all but impossible Under the law, 80% of the cityâs public school classrooms must not exceed 20-25 students (depending on the grade level) by this coming school year. All schools must meet the caps by the 2027-28 school year. That goal may be out of reach: Schools Chancellor Kamar Samuels recently told City Council members it would be âvery difficultâ to hit 80% compliance by September. The city was only able to hit this yearâs 60% benchmark by exempting thousands of classes from the law, despite spending hundreds of millions hiring more teachers and helping schools reconfigure space. Failing to meet the benchmarks has big consequences. The law includes a provision that calls for hundreds of millions in state funding cuts if the city does not comply. Even the lawâs biggest supporters, however, likely want to avoid that outcome. A delay gives Mamdani some (temporary) budget relief Mamdani is looking for all the savings he can to close a $5.4 billion budget gap by the cityâs July 1 budget deadline. The mayor is banking on a delay of the class size law to help balance the cityâs budget, the New York Times reported last week. (A City Hall spokesperson did not respond to questions about the mayorâs push for a delay.) But having more time to comply with the law might only bring a temporary reprieve. Liu has floated requiring the city to bring 70% of classrooms into compliance this coming September â instead of 80% â with an additional 10% in each of the three following school years. The state lawmaker pushed back against any move by the Mamdani administration to use the delay to save money. âAdjustments in the timeline are not meant to provide fiscal relief,â Liu told Chalkbeat, noting that any extension is contingent on the city submitting more detailed plans about how they plan to comply with the law. The Mamdani administration deserves more time to comply because the city is âbehind the curve due to the inactions of the previous administration,â Liu added. The cityâs teachers union, a key backer of the class size mandate, said they want to see a clearer plan from the Education Department to build more classroom space before supporting changes to the law. âOnly when a real plan and a clear timeline are in place â not the usual School Construction [Authority] smoke and mirrors â would we have the information necessary to consider whether any amendments are needed,â United Federation of Teachers President Michael Mulgrew said in a statement. The mayorâs preliminary budget includes $543 million in additional city funding to reduce class sizes next fiscal year and $943 million in each of the three fiscal years after that â spending that could be pushed back if the legislature changes the timeline for implementing class size reductions. (City officials have previously projected the cost of fully implementing the law could be up to $1.7 billion a year for additional teachers with billions more in school construction costs on top of that.) In its response to Mamdaniâs preliminary budget, City Council indicated the city should convince state lawmakers to pony up for funding to comply with the law, relieving the city of some costs. The state assembly proposed sending the city $600 million to shrink classes, but itâs unclear if that will be included in the final state budget deal. But in the mayorâs response criticizing the City Councilâs plan, he accused them of seeking a delay to the class size law as their sole request from Albany â an ask that does not appear in the councilâs budget proposal. Some observers noted that itâs a bizarre critique coming from Mamdani. âIsnât [Mamdani] also asking the state for more time to lower class size?â Leonie Haimson, the executive director of the advocacy group Class Size Matters, wrote on X. A Mamdani spokesperson did not respond to questions about his false characterization of the councilâs budget response. City may want more time to come up with a comprehensive plan to shrink classes Some of the lawâs backers have urged the city to come up with more aggressive plans to build new classrooms for crowded schools that donât have room to comply with the law. But there are other solutions the city could use in some cases that would be far less expensive and take advantage of empty seats elsewhere: capping enrollment on crowded campuses or rezonings. Samuels has also signaled he wants to prioritize using the cityâs existing buildings before turning to school construction, though moves to cap enrollment on popular campuses would likely face pushback from families. âThe time in theory gives [Mamdani] the opportunity to get creative about space utilization [and] enrollment,â said Jonathan Collins, a political science and education professor at Columbia Universityâs Teachers College and a member of the cityâs Panel for Educational Policy. A delay could also give the city more time to grapple with one of the lawâs core equity challenges: It requires significant new spending at relatively more affluent schools, where class sizes tend to be bigger, while more high-poverty schools already meet the caps. A recent report from the Urban Institute, a think tank, found that the cityâs lowest-poverty schools have received more funding to reduce class sizes than the cityâs highest-poverty schools. Alex Zimmerman is a senior reporter for Chalkbeat New York, covering NYC public schools. Contact Alex at azimmerman@chalkbeat.org.

articles.title: Tips for Families to Support Nutritional Health & Wellness
articles.author: Dr Greg Carevale MD
articles.article_text: --- title: Tips for Families to Support Nutritional Health & Wellness author: Dr Greg Carevale MD url: https://www.ourtownny.com/features/healthy-manhattan/tips-for-families-to-support-nutritional-health-wellness-MD5720086 hostname: ourtownny.com description: Good nutrition is about more than maintaining a healthy weight; it promotes healthy aging and helps combat diseases such as heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and ... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-03 tags: ['dr. greg carevale md', 'health& fitness'] --- Tips for Families to Support Nutritional Health & Wellness Lots of factors figure into good health beyond simply exercise and weight. Here a leading doctor gives his view on the topic and points out that it is not the same for all people. Good nutrition is about more than maintaining a healthy weight; it promotes healthy aging and helps combat diseases such as heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and obesity. Despite the benefits of a healthy lifestyle, only 7.4% of adults nationally and 12% in New York reported eating two or more fruits or three or more vegetables a day. Prioritizing nutrient-rich foods, such as a variety of fruits and vegetables, healthy proteins like nuts and fish, and whole-grain fiber, while limiting processed foods and added sugars is essential for overall wellness. A nutritious diet does not have to look the same for everyone. The Dietary Guidelines for Americans purposely provides recommendations based on food groups and subgroups â not specific foods and beverages â to allow for customization and make food their own by selecting healthy foods, beverages, and snacks that work for individualsâ personal preferences. Here are some tips for individuals to help promote strong nutritional health: Encourage healthy eating from a young age. According to the CDC, fewer than 1 in 10 children and adults eat the recommended daily amount of vegetables, and only 4 in 10 meet fruit recommendations. A great way to ensure children receive a nutritionally balanced, healthy, and appealing meal is to pack a healthy school lunch. Swap out added sugars, such as those found in grain-based desserts, breakfast cereals, yogurts, and flavored milks, with fresh-cut fruits and veggies, whole grains, seafood, beans, low-fat dairy products, or lean meats and poultry. Limit or avoid foods high in added sugar, carbohydrates, and saturated fat. Most foods can fit into a healthy eating pattern when consumed in moderation. However, according to the USDAâs Dietary Guidelines, some foods and beverages that are higher in added sugar, saturated fat, and sodium should be limited, including: Alcoholic beverages: If adults of legal drinking age choose to drink alcohol, they should limit their intake to two drinks per day for men or one drink or less per day for women. Some people should avoid drinking alcohol altogether, including women who are pregnant and those with certain health conditions. Sodium: Adults should consume less than 2,300 milligrams per dayâeven less for children under 14.3 High sodium content is often found in highly processed foods and should be avoided.Added sugars: Added sugars can be hard to spot. To help identify sources of added sugars, look for ingredients that include the word âsugarâ or âsyrupâ or end in â-ose.â Saturated fat: Healthy fats from meats, poultry, and eggs are essential in a balanced diet; however, saturated fat intake should not exceed 10% of total daily calories.3 Understand the most important guidelines: Food and diet can be customized to reflect personal preferences, budgetary restrictions and cultural traditions. The USDA recommends that Americans meet their nutritional needs through consuming nutrient-dense foods and beverages and staying within daily calorie limits. Ultimately, choosing nutrient-rich foods should be the focus when deciding what to eat. Most of the calories (approximately 85 percent) a person consumes in a day come from nutrient-rich foods; only a small amount (approximately 15 percent) is left over for saturated fats, sugar, or alcoholic beverages. Where to begin. While making any major lifestyle change can feel overwhelming, incorporating more nutritious foods does not have to be challenging. Recognizing that the foods and beverages we consume have a profound effect on our health is a great place to start. The scientific connection between food and health is well documented, and there is substantial evidence that healthy dietary patterns can help people achieve and maintain good health and reduce the risk of chronic diseases at all stages of life. Encouraging healthy eating habits from a young age and staying consistent by prioritizing a nutrient-rich lifestyle are key to living a longer, healthier life. Using online resources, such as the Dietary Guidelines for Americans, can help you decide what to eat and drink to support a healthy eating routine. Dr. Greg Carnevale is the chief medical officer of UnitedHealthcare of New York.

articles.title: City Council Must Pass Bill to Ensure Home-Delivered Meals for Older Adults
articles.author: Yakov Keiserman
articles.article_text: --- title: City Council Must Pass Bill to Ensure Home-Delivered Meals for Older Adults author: Yakov Keiserman url: https://www.ourtownny.com/voices/city-council-must-pass-bill-to-ensure-home-delivered-meals-for-older-adults-KA5727804 hostname: ourtownny.com description: I used to see him in the hallway of our apartment building from time to time. He lived alone after his wife passed, just next door to me. He kept to himself. A ... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-03 tags: ['voices', 'yakov keiserman'] --- City Council Must Pass Bill to Ensure Home-Delivered Meals for Older Adults The writer tells of the challenges older adults faceâespecially when living alone and facing food insecurities. He is urging the City Council to pass Intro 0280-2026 which would ensure meal service for older adults seven days a week. I used to see him in the hallway of our apartment building from time to time. He lived alone after his wife passed, just next door to me. He kept to himself. A few times, I invited him to come with me to the theater, but he always declined. When the elevator in our building was out, as it often was, Iâd help him carry his groceries up the stairs to the fourth floor where we both lived. I thought to myself at times: how would he manage if I couldnât help him? We had these small, passing interactions, but I never once saw the inside of his apartment. I remember thinking his days must have been long and quiet. Then one day, he was gone. I stopped seeing him, and after a while, I realized my neighbor must have passed on. There was no announcement, no gathering, nothing to mark his passing. Just the absence of someone whom I had become used to seeing for many years. And this is the sad reality for too many older New Yorkers. I often think back to himâhow he managed day to day, and whether he always had what he needed, including something as basic as food. Today, more than 1.8 million New Yorkers are over the age of 60, a number that continues to grow every year. In Manhattan, tens of thousands of older adults live alone, many of them homebound and at risk of food insecurity and social isolation. As the cityâs aging population grows, so will its needs. And while this reality is known to many of our leaders, the system falls short in a fundamental way. Our city currently funds home-delivered meals for homebound older adults only five days per week. That policy suggests that older adults donât need to eat on weekends. And itâs a serious, dangerous failure to the folks who built this city we currently enjoy. Hunger does not keep a weekday schedule. Malnutrition does not take Saturdays and Sundays off. For older adults who are homebound or living with chronic illness, missing meals can quickly spiral into serious health consequences like hospitalization, accelerated decline, and even death. We are talking about people who raised families here, worked here, paid taxes here, and shaped the communities we now call home. And yet, during whatâs supposed to be their golden years in one of the wealthiest cities in the world, we are effectively telling them that five days of food is enough. When older adults have consistent access to nutritious meals, they stay healthier and more independent, with the fuel they need to enjoy their lives. Home-delivered meals are essential to the survival of so many older adults in our city. For many, it is the only regular human interaction they have. A knock on the door. A quick check-in. A moment of recognition. The City Council has an opportunity to address this in its next session. Passing Bill Intro 0280-2026 would ensure home-delivered meals are available for older adults seven days a week, 365 days a year. No one in New York City should go hungry. And no one should disappear without being noticed. Yakov Keiserman is an older adult and a member of Encore Community Services.

articles.title: Anxiety Rises as Deadline Looms to Pick Penn Master Planner
articles.author: Michael Oreskes
articles.article_text: --- title: Anxiety Rises as Deadline Looms to Pick Penn Master Planner author: Michael Oreskes url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/anxiety-rises-as-deadline-looms-to-pick-penn-master-planner-ME5723691 hostname: ourtownny.com description: After years of squabbling and stasis over the future of Penn Station and its neighborhood, Amtrak will soon make one of the most significant development decisio... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-03 tags: ['michael oreskes', 'penn station'] --- Anxiety Rises as Deadline Looms to Pick Penn Master Planner The deadline for three contenders to submit their bids to Amtrak to become the master developer for the multi-billion-dollar Penn Station development plan is May 4. Local activists and political leaders are pushing for more transparency in the planning process. After years of squabbling and stasis over the future of Penn Station and its neighborhood, Amtrak will soon make one of the most significant development decisions in the modern history of New York. The passenger railroad, which owns Penn Station and is in turn owned by the federal government, says that by June it will announce the selection of a Master Developer to be its partner in âtransformingâ the infamously gritty commuter hub into a world class destination. As that ambition suggests, picking the Master developer is not like hiring a contractor to redo your kitchen. Amtrak is not just selecting a builder. It is picking a planâa plan that will alter the very fabric of midtown Manhattan. Which is why there is increasing restlessness among elected officials, community activists and transit advocates that the selection is being made behind closed doors. âMy neighbors and I believe we should know which plans, if any, are saying they need to demolish our neighborhood,â said Gene Sinigalliano, leader of the Tenants Association at 251 West 30th street, a building that would be in the path of any plan that expanded the station southward. âWe are steadfastly opposed to this and any other plans that are presenting the demolition of our neighborhood. We want the ability to voice these concerns on a fully informed basis before any decisions are made.â Sinigallianoâs anxiety does not seem to have been allayed by either Governor Hochulâs statement that she would not countenance the destruction of his neighborhood or by the assurance from Amtrakâs man on the scene, Andy Byford that a southern expansion is for now on hold pending study of the expansion issue. All he knows for certain, Sinigalliano explains, is that one of the three finalists for the master developer job, Grand Penn Partners, had presented plans that included an expansion onto his block. That presentation was before Amtrak imposed a blackout on all statements by the contenders. So Sinigalliano has no way to know if expansion remains Grand Pennâs plan or, even if it is, whether Amtrak would select them. Expansion of the station is only one of several contentious issues: *Good government advocates worry about the overall cost of the project and who, ultimately, will pay. *Residents and community officials wonder whether the project will revive a version of Governor Andrew Cuomoâs plan for commercial development around the station to generate development revenue to help fund the station project. *Transit advocates say the Penn project is a golden opportunity to reinvent how the Long Island Railroad and New Jersey Transit operate so their trains run through the station, rather than dropping all their riders there. *And the Grand Penn consortium stirred a bunch of news coverage in recent days by hints that maybe, just maybe, the owner of Madison Square Garden was open to their idea to move the Garden to rebuild the station with a park on top. The owner, James Dolan, has said nothing himself to recant his earlier comments that he had no desire to move and Byford, while never shutting the door on moving the Garden, has noted it would drag out the reconstruction timeline, which federal officials like to brag is going at âthe speed of Trump.â Any one of these would be a topic for major public debate. To be sure, there has been much discussion and the general plans of two of the bidders, Grand Penn and Halmar/ASTM, have long been public. But to drive home the point of how much is not known, a third bidder, led by the Canadian developer Fengate Capital, entered late and has said not a public word about its proposals. There is also the basic question of who exactly has the authority to do what beyond the property Amtrak actually owns, which is the station below street level and a powerhouse just across West 31st Street, which backs up on Gene Sinigallianoâs apartment building. The Cuomo Penn District redevelopment plan would have used the stateâs power of condemnation to override city zoning and build supertall office towers. But Amtrak has not described what authority it would invoke, or recruit from the state, for any part of its plan that went outside the four corners of the station property it owns. A group of elected officials weighed in the other day in a letter to Byford. They are pressing Amtrak to release the instructions, its Request for Proposal, that shapes the negotiations currently underway with the three finalists for Master Developer. Amtrak has said its procurement rules preclude releasing the RFP, but the elected officials poured cold water on that claim. âWe request a written response identifying the specific legal basis, if any, for withholding the RFP from public disclosure. In the absence of any such legal basis we would urge you to release the RFP to the public.â The demand was signed by the elected officials whose districts cover Penn Station and its neighborhood: Rep Jerrold Nadler; Borough President Brad Hoylman-Sigal; State Senators Liz Krueger and Erik Bottcher; Assemblymember Tony Simone. In a letter Friday to the elected officials, Byford again rejected their request for the RFP. But he sought to calm the larger concerns by saying the selection of the Master Developer was only the start of the process. No final decisions will be made on the financing or design of the station before ârobust public engagementâ starting this summer. The RFP is unquestionably a vital document, which Amtrak says it canât release because it would compromise competitive secrecy. But the reality is that most of the demands for more information are not about Amtrakâs requests but rather about the proposals from the three bidders. âWe believe more should be done to inform the public of the shape this project may be taking before a final decision is made,â said Samuel Turvey of ReThinkNYC, a prominent advocate. âPenn Station, when combined with the Gateway Tunnels project, is the largest infrastructure project in the country and one of the largest such projects in our history and has generated tremendous public interest. There are questions that we believe could be answered at some time before a final decision is made.â Turvey proposed that Amtrak and the three bidders answer publicly in âexecutive summary fashionâ key questions on their plans. Among these questions, he said, are: âBasics of how a proponent will fund the project (admittedly easier said than done) ... Does the proponent provide protections of any sort for cost overruns? Does the proponent look to demolish any of the neighborhood in order to accomplish their goals? âDoes the proponent have a position on through-running and whether that could happen within the confines of the existing station or would such a conversion require physical expansion of the station? Does the proponent seek to ask the government to use eminent domain to clear the way for commercial development that can help fund the project?â Amtrak argues that it has offered extensive information about its goals, including at public appearances by Byford and regular briefings to a Station Working Advisory Group that includes, among others, those elected officials and activists and advocates. The three finalists are required to submit their final bid to Amtrak on May 4. âThe bid will be evaluated against strict criteria and by a panel (that includes me) that has had no contact with bidders whatsoever,â Byford explained. âEach bid will be judged strictly on its merits.â A winner will be confirmed by the Amtrak Board in June.

articles.title: A True Village Old-School Original Withstands the Test of Time
articles.author: Karen Rempel
articles.article_text: --- title: A True Village Old-School Original Withstands the Test of Time author: Karen Rempel url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/a-true-village-old-school-original-withstands-the-test-of-time-IA5727291 hostname: ourtownny.com description: Does the name Reminiscence ring a bell? Whether youâre a long-time New York resident, a visitor, or an NYU student who frequented the Villageâs groovy hippie sc... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-02 tags: ['karen rempel'] --- A True Village Old-School Original Withstands the Test of Time After fifty years, Reminiscence is still going strong. The Fifth Avenue store features vintage clothing, affordable fashion, and quirky gifts. Does the name Reminiscence ring a bell? Whether youâre a long-time New York resident, a visitor, or an NYU student who frequented the Villageâs groovy hippie scene in the seventies, chances are you still treasure a tie-dyed shirt or denim skirt from Reminiscenceâs original location at 175 MacDougal Street. In fact, if you came on the New York scene at any time in the past five decades, you may have stumbled upon this unique Manhattan source for fun affordable fashion. Picture the scene. A beautiful early summer day, 1975. Seventh Avenue South is Hippie Central. Love and peace, people giving out flowers. A young woman is street-peddling rice prints from India. Sheâs wearing a unique, eye-catching denim jacket she embroidered while hitchhiking across America and Canada. A young entrepreneur whoâs peddling his denim clothing sees her and asks her if she can make clothes like that for him. At that moment, a five-decades-and-counting business friendship was born. The woman was Joan Bedor, and the man was Stewart Richer. Stewart had a denim warehouse in Chelsea, with 1000-pound bales of every shade of denim from baby blue to midnight. Joan had been in love with denim fabrics since she was a girl studying art in Highbridge, Bronx. She used to show her friends how to stone-wash denim with actual stones. They were a perfect match. Joan started making denim skirts for Stewart, carrying armfuls of fabric around the city all that summer. After a few months of making and peddling clothes together, Stewart decided to open a store. He found a place for rent for $1,000 a month on MacDougal Street. He had saved up $3,000 to put into the business. Joan became his store manager, as well as co-creator. In addition to designing and making denim clothing, they bought Army and Navy surplus clothing and dyed it, then sold it âsuper cheapâ to customers that were lined up out the door throughout the late seventies and into the mid-eighties, when Stewart bought a new storefront on 74 Fifth Avenue, where it still stands. The Reminiscence brand spread. In the eighties they sold wholesale clothing to stores throughout America, as well as to five Mitsubishi-owned Muji clothing locations in Japan. Their clothing was so unique and popular that Reminiscence won a Coty American Fashion Criticsâ Special Award for unusual clothing in 1980. Joan also put her creative talents to designing shopping bags for the store. The bagâs shape was inspired by a drawstring bag called âthe banana bagâ that sheâd seen on a buying trip in Italy. For many springs and falls, she created unique art designs reflecting her travels and the fashion scene. Joanâs bags are featured in Shopping Bag Design 2: Creative Promotional Graphics by Judi Radice. Joan recalls, âPeople used to carry them around New York. Now people collect them... People come in and say ,âI still have a Reminiscence bag!ââ As the decades roll by, Reminiscence is always deliberately behind the times. Thereâs something mysterious about the store that can camouflage it from passersby. But once you stumble across it and enter the time portal thatâs set a few feet back from the sidewalk, you never forget it. Joan said, âPeople come from all over. When they visit New York, they have memories of when they shopped here when they were NYU students. People like to come backâitâs old New York.â But if vintage hippie clothing is not your bag (sorry), you might be a tech bro or bra drawn in to get your duds for Burning Man. Rodney Rufo has been creating the storeâs window displays for 35 years. For his most recent display, mounted in March, he said, âI wanted to bring some color because itâs been so gloomy in the city.â Rodney designs seasonal displays for Christmas and Halloween, and he designs a special Burning Man window in July, for the late August event. Itâs a busy time for the store, when Manhattanites plan their annual pilgrimage to the desert. Reminiscence is strictly old-school. No website. No telephone. Hand-written receipts in triplicate. Thereâs something very peaceful about the vibe in the store. Joan maintains the peace and love vibe deliberately. Unless youâre a shoplifterâshe will spot you instantly due to her growing-up-in-the-Bronx street radar! In that case, no quarter will be given. This story marks the seventh anniversary of this column about the quirky clothes, places, and people in New York. For a lark, check out my first Karenâs Quirky Style column, featuring Leopard-prints a-leaping! For me, it brings back memories of wonderful friends and a very spotty evening! Thanks to all of you for reading, and my deep appreciation to the creative commenters who share remarks to add to the fun. RIP, Jeff. I will miss your contributions and friendship. Style Notes These vintage seventies shades from Reminiscence make me look exactly like my red-headed Aunt Lynne spiking the punch at her backyard wedding reception, circa 1977. The beaded jewelry and Reminiscence crocheted shawl add to the seventies vibe, with the foundational Donna Karan beaded tank and Dries Van Noten snakeskin boots laying the platform. Karen Rempel is a New York-based writer, model, and artist. For past stories, see https://karenqs.nyc.

articles.title: Yorkville NYCHA Tenants Vote Against NYCHA-Favored Funding Switch
articles.author: Jack Ahern
articles.article_text: --- title: Yorkville NYCHA Tenants Vote Against NYCHA-Favored Funding Switch author: Jack Ahern url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/yorkville-nycha-tenants-vote-against-nycha-favored-funding-switch-EA5727006 hostname: ourtownny.com description: The residents of a Yorkville public housing complex voted to reject the adoption of a new management model pushed by NYCHA itself last month, instead choosing t... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-06 tags: ['nycha', 'jack ahern', 'isaacs houses'] --- Yorkville NYCHA Tenants Vote Against NYCHA-Favored Funding Switch The residents of the Isaacs Houses on East 93rd Street voted to stick with Section 9 funding last monthârather than move to one of two Section 8-based models, including one highly favored by NYCHA itself. The residents of a Yorkville public housing complex voted to reject the adoption of a new management model pushed by NYCHA itself last month, instead choosing to stick with Section 9 federal funding. It was the first vote of its kind in Manhattan, and the eighth citywide. Specifically, Isaacs Houses residents on East 93rd Street voted against joining either the New York City Public Housing Preservation Trust or the RAD-PACT Program. In the final tally, 309 out of 526 eligible voters expressed their desire to maintain the status quo. The 30-day vote process was overseen by MK Elections Services. A total of 209 voters voted to pivot towards the Trust, while a meager 12 pitched for RAD-PACT. The latter would have involved bringing in a private landlord to manage the Isaacs Houses, which would still be owned by NYCHA. Both involve Section 8 subsidies. Residents who publicly backed leaving things unchanged, such as âSave Section 9â campaigner Sandra Coleman, expressed fear that any switch could provide a backdoor to higher eviction rates or further privatization. Rose Bergin, the Resident Leader of the Isaacs Houses, wanted the Trust to win out. She told Our Town that she believed the vote to stick with Section 9 was âutterly ridiculous,â and thought that Coleman had âfrightenedâ residents into sticking with what she views as a far inferior option. Section 8 funding, Bergin said, could have provided money to stem leaks and fix up the boiler system. The vote count for switching to RAD-PACT remained utterly unchanged between an early vote count in mid-March and the final tally on March 24, while the Preservation Trust grew relatively more competitive, after starting out at a count of only 80 votes. The Trust model has been heavily promoted by NYCHA itself. It was created in 2022 as a public benefit corporation that can issue floating bonds for outstanding NYCHA repairs, which NYCHA itself canât do. Currently, Section 8 subsidies provide more funding on balance than Section 9 doesâdue to federal spending decisionsâwhich NYCHA cites as one reason for its support of the Trust model. After the Isaacs Houses vote, the agency issued a statement expressing measured disappointment. âWhile the option [residents] selected will not unlock the necessary funding to complete comprehensive renovations to the development, we are resolute in our dedication and service to all of the residents who call Isaacs home,â NYCHA Chief Executive Officer Lisa Bova-Hiatt said. CIty Hall appears to have been pulling for a switch to the Trust, too, as a statement by Deputy Mayor for Housing and Planning Leila Borzog made clear. âFive hundred and twenty-six out of 869 eligible Isaacs Houses residents made their voices heard in this election,â she said. âOf those, 309 voters chose to remain in the Section 9 funding system rather than convert to the more stable Section 8 system with a path to comprehensive renovations.â âI appreciate all who came out to participate in a decision that impacts the future of their communityâresident voices must remain at the center of our work to deliver for NYCHA residents,â Borzog added. NYCHA would have remained the permanent owner of the Isaacs Houses under the Trust arrangement, but would have entered into a ground-lease arrangement with the Trust. Both the Housing Preservation Trust and RAD-PACT involve leveraging said Section 8 funding, which provides repair subsidies to specific apartments (hence âproject-basedâ), such as those in the Isaacs Houses. Longstanding Section 9 funding, meanwhile, involves providing direct federal funding to public housing authorities such as NYCHA. The Housing Preservation Trust, as a technically separate entity from NYHCA, can unlock Section 8 via the aforementioned lease arrangement. RAD-PACT does so through private management. If the Isaacs Houses residents had voted to make the switch to the Trust, certain unions who maintain a project agreement with NYCHA would have made repairs to the building. Under RAD-PACT, non-union labor could have been hired to do so. Plumbers Local 1 and the District Council of Carpenters canvassed at the public housing complex in late Jan., as the vote was ongoing, encouraging residents to vote for the Trust.

articles.title: Teen Finally Home after Falling Down Shaft on Queensboro Bridge
articles.author: Keith J Kelly
articles.article_text: --- title: Teen Finally Home after Falling Down Shaft on Queensboro Bridge author: Keith J Kelly url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/teen-finally-home-after-falling-down-shaft-on-queensboro-bridge-KF5722804 hostname: ourtownny.com description: A 16-year-old boy who was critically injured after he fell down a 50 ft shaft on the Queensboro Bridge and was abandoned by his companions arrived home on Long ... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-03 tags: ['fdny', 'keith j. kelly', 'queensboro bridge', 'teen bridge fall'] --- Teen Finally Home after Falling Down Shaft on Queensboro Bridge Three teens, who abandoned the 16-year-old victim after he fell 50 ft. down a workerâs maintenance shaft while they were in midst of making a video, were arrested in the days after the near-fatal Feb. 16 incident. A 16-year-old boy who was critically injured after he fell down a 50 ft shaft on the Queensboro Bridge and was abandoned by his companions arrived home on Long Island recently after spending up to a month in the hospital recovering from injuries suffered in the fall. News that the victim, Frankie Allocca, had arrived home was posted on a GoFundMe site by his mother, Vanessa Tineo, who thanked supporters in a post on March 15, nearly a month after the near fatal accident on Feb. 16. She gave no update on his condition beyond saying he was ânow homeâ and âjust needs some time to heal completely.â In the days after her son was rescued by firefighters, the anguished mom, in an interview with Gothamist, said the companions of her son that fateful day âleft him to die.â Firefighters spent hours extracting the injured teen from the maintenance shaft that he was exploring with several other teen âurban explorersâ when he apparently slipped off a ladder and fell to the bottom of the cold dark shaft. A police dispatcher on the night of the rescue described him as a âcode red patientâ which generally describes a person in a life threatening emergency situation. EMS rushed the teen to NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medical Center where he was said to be in stable condition. But his injuries were such that he was going to have to learn to walk again, according to a social media post by a family friend. In a Whatâs App video reviewed by the NY Post, Frankie can be heard screaming, âmy leg, my leg!â but none of the companions entered the shaft to try to save him. âBro, Frankie is dead. Frankie is fâking dead. Oh my God. No, no, no,â one of the companions can be heard saying after they climbed back to the bridge roadway. . Family friend David Darcy, who started the GoFundMe that raised $20,738 as of April 2 said all of Frankieâs âfriendsâ fled without notifying authorities. âNo one deserves to be abandoned in their worst moment,â Darcy wrote. âInstead of helping him, they left him there,â Darcy wrote. âThey even took his phone and disposed of it, leaving him with no way to call for help.â Darcy said it took first responders four hours to locate the injured 16-year-old. âFrankie lay alone for over four hours, bleeding and exposed to the cold,â Darcy wrote. âBy the time help arrived, he had lost a severe amount of blood and was hypothermic. âDoctors have said he is expected to live and walk again. His survival is nothing short of a miracle,â Darcy wrote on GoFundMe in the days after the incident. A 17-year-old girl who joined a Whatâs App discussion that included some of the companions who had fled, called police when she realized nobody on the bridge with Frankie reported the accident. First responders answered the call but were initially not able to find him. They returned later and he was finally able to be hoisted from the dark, shaft nearly six hours after he fell. His mother, Vanessa Tineo, made a post on the GoFundMe page heralding the news that her son was home, but made no further update on his physical condition in a post on March 15. âWe want to thank everyone for your love and support,â wrote Tineo, his mom. âThank you all very much for the kind words and donations. Frankie is doing well now home just needs some time to heal completely. One day at a time!!â At least three teens were arrested in the days after the incident. A 14-year-old boy was arrested on Feb. 17 and charged with criminal trespass. A 15-year-old, who turned himself into the 20th Pct on Feb. 18, was charged with criminal endangerment and criminal trespass. A third 14-year-old was also arrested on Feb. 20 and charged with criminal endangerment and criminal trespass. Criminal endangerment could be a felony if it also involved depraved indifference to human life. It could not be determined if the two who were facing criminal endangerment counts were hit with misdemeanor or felony charges. The names of the three defendants were not released due to their ages and the cases fell under the jurisdiction of Family Court rather than the Manhattan District Attorney.

articles.title: Two Cab Drivers Celebrate Debut in Indie Film âLove New Yorkâ
articles.author: Sarah Gassel
articles.article_text: --- title: Two Cab Drivers Celebrate Debut in Indie Film âLove New Yorkâ author: Sarah Gassel url: https://www.ourtownny.com/city-arts/two-cab-drivers-celebrate-debut-in-indie-film-love-new-york-ID5720309 hostname: ourtownny.com description: A full house packed into a private screening of the 2025 indie dark comedy âLove New York,â a reflection of the unique modern-day social and romantic interactio... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-02 tags: ['seth goldman', 'john mcdonagh', 'irish repertory theater', 'sarah gassel', 'love new york', 'anthony dimieri'] --- Two Cab Drivers Celebrate Debut in Indie Film âLove New Yorkâ Yellow cab drivers John McDonagh and Seth Goldman hosted a private screening of the dark comedy for friends and family at the Irish Repertory Theatre. A full house packed into a private screening of the 2025 indie dark comedy âLove New York,â a reflection of the unique modern-day social and romantic interactions that take place in New York City. As viewers attentively watched at the Irish Repertory Theatre in Chelsea, they were taken through the scenes of a painful first date with a social media influencer and into a relationship being unraveled by a political debate in Domino Park. The film soon led to Tompkins Square Parkâ the scene that the audience had been waiting for. As the screen revealed two old friends sitting on a park bench, a round of cheering and applause set off in the audience. The two men, John McDonagh and Seth Goldman, hosted the screening on March 25 to commemorate their debut into the film world. Bouts of laughter filled the theatre as their friends and family watched the two bicker over a couple kissing across from them. âThis is a two-year adventure Seth and I have been on,â McDonagh said. Goldman and McDonagh have each spent over 35 years as yellow cab drivers. The two met filmmaker Anthony DiMieri, the director and producer of âLove New York,â at a diner. DiMieri says watching the two interact was magical, and he cast them in the roles in the fall of 2024. McDonagh plays the cynic of the pair and complains to Goldmanâs character about the very public display of affection in front of them. Goldman, however, counters his perspective and talks about the joys of young love. Their relationship adds some needed comic relief and a new layer of authenticity to the film. âIt writes itself,â DiMieri said. âI didnât really have to do much work.â DiMieri, who wrote the script, mainly developed the scenes around his observations of the archetypes in New York City. âThe story was inspired by lots of true stories,â he said. The entirety of the plot takes place in a 24-hour time period. Each scene is introduced by movement as the film travels through several boroughs and neighborhoods. A new set of characters is built from the filmâs ensemble cast, reflecting the different tropes of the city, ranging from the newer finance bros to the curmudgeonly old school native New Yorkers, as played by McDonagh and Goldman. âLove New York,â which premiered in October of last year at the 2025 Downtown Film Festival, has since won best feature film at both the Bowery Film Festival and the New York Comedy Film Festival. For several of the audience members, it was their second time seeing the film, including CiarÃ¡n OâReilly, the founder and director of the Irish Rep. âItâs an impressionateâs view of what itâs like to live in New York,â he said. âItâs a mosaic.â After the screening, Goldman, McDonagh, and Tim Sarmiento, a producer on the film, joined the stage for a Q&A moderated by OâReilly. DiMieri, who was traveling back from Rome, also phoned in, aptly from the back of a yellow cab. The five discussed the film as a whole as well as some of the individual scenes. OâReilly expressed his admiration for one scene of the first date between a sugar baby and an older man simply looking for company. In the film, the man, played by Ezra Barnes, tells the woman he is on the board of the Museum of Modern Art after she reveals she is a painter. Barnes, who was in the crowd, stood up. âIf anyone wants a free tour of the water lilies,â Barnes began jokingly. âDonât ask me. Iâm really not on the board.â McDonagh and Goldman also addressed what acting in their first film was like. â35 years driving a yellow cab in New York City, I was an experienced actor,â Goldman joked. Though this is the pairâs first film roles, it is not the only time they have been cast by DiMieri, who is also the co-founder of Melted Solids, the creative agency credited with helping elect Mayor Zohran Mamdani. In December 2024, they stepped into the limelight when they starred in an early campaign ad for Mamdani, a then-unknown mayoral candidate. Across social media and YouTube, the ad has garnered over 20 million views. Since then, they have continued to star in the social media posts of the viral NYC content creators Kareem Rahma, who produces SubwayTakes, and Nicolas Heller, who produces the show Survival of the Fittest. The journey inspired McDonagh, who is also a playwright, to produce the one-man show âTaxi 2 TikTok,â which he debuted on March 28 at the New York Society for Ethical Culture. After the Q&A, the audience was invited to celebrate at The Copper Still in Chelsea, where they packed out the bar area. Some of the group were relatives or met the two through the film. Others know them through their various pursuits, with Goldman being a writer and McDonagh a radio host and activist. âThis was all done by word of mouth,â McDonagh said of the event. It was one more vignette of the city.

articles.title: Make Giant Pigeon Statue on High Line a Permanent Exhibit: Petition
articles.author: Mary Mayo
articles.article_text: --- title: Make Giant Pigeon Statue on High Line a Permanent Exhibit: Petition author: Mary Mayo url: https://www.ourtownny.com/news/make-giant-pigeon-statue-on-high-line-a-permanent-exhibit-petition-EB5715413 hostname: ourtownny.com description: NYC may be waving goodbye to its giant pigeon statue, but not without a fight. Located in Manhattanâs Chelsea neighborhood along the High Line, Ivan Argoteâs sc... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-04-02 tags: ['mary mayo', 'pigeon statute'] --- Make Giant Pigeon Statue on High Line a Permanent Exhibit: Petition The giant 17-foot-tall High Line sculpture is scheduled to come down sometime in April. A petition is circulating asking the Parks Dept to make the sculpture a permanent exhibit. NYC may be waving goodbye to its giant pigeon statue, but not without a fight. Located in Manhattanâs Chelsea neighborhood along the High Line, Ivan Argoteâs sculpture titled âDinosaurâ has quickly become a fan favorite for both tourists and NYC locals. Since 2024, the 17 foot aluminum pigeon has served as a homage to the cityâs most ubiquitous bird. However, due to the High Lineâs rotating public art program, its time in the park is limited. After 18 months on display, the sculpture is scheduled to be removed sometime this spring to make way for the next scheduled installation, a 27 foot sandstone Buddha sculpture titled âThe Light That Shines Through the Universe.â Many argue that the sculpture now stands as a cultural symbol to the city sparking a current petition advocating for âDinosaurâ to become a permanent installation. âIâm heartbroken about this pigeon,â said Julia Harris, visiting the monument in its last moments. âItâs very unique... pigeons are overlooked, seen as dirty or like trash. I feel like this commemorates that thereâs beauty to them.â Harris is among the thousands who have signed a petition circulating on Change.org calling on the city to make the exhibit permanent. Over 7,000 people have signed the petition as of March 31. âKeep the pigeon, itâs very New York,â said Harris. The publicâs love and adoration for âDinosaurâ and dismay for its departure has not gone unnoticed. A farewell event, titled âFarewell Dinosaurâ held on March 21, drew large crowds from around the city to celebrate the towering piegon. Attendees enjoyed pigeon themed activities including DJ sets, trivia, bingo, and limited edition posters signed by the artist, IvÃ¡n Argote. Argote is a Colombian artist who is now dubbed as the youngest and first from the global south to receive a High Line Plinth commission. Argote has described the sculpture as a way to elevate the often slandered pigeon. Pigeons were originally domesticated and commemorated for their role as military messengers during the 1800âs. Now, many see them as a nuisance. Weighing around 2,000 pounds and hand painted for hyper-realistic details, âDinosaurâ has prompted New Yorkers to rethink their relationship with the birds that share their sidewalks. The petition reads: âthe pigeon is a symbol of resilience, perseverance, and represents NYC and all it stands for. Removing the sculpture would eliminate a piece of art that has already become deeply meaningful to the community in a short period of time.â But so far, the petition drive has not prompted the High Line to reverse itself. The sculpture is expected to leave sometime in April.

articles.title: H. G. Wellsâs âThe Croquet Playerâ Plays at East Village Theater
articles.author: Bianca Lopez
articles.article_text: --- title: H. G. Wellsâs âThe Croquet Playerâ Plays at East Village Theater author: Bianca Lopez url: https://www.ourtownny.com/city-arts/h-g-wells-s-the-croquet-player-plays-at-east-village-theater-BN5719245 hostname: ourtownny.com description: There is something inherently alluring about the unpredictability of a show at Theater for the New City. Bold, gritty, and daring theater sets it apart from the... sitename: www.ourtownny.com date: 2026-03-31 tags: ['east village', 'off broadway theater', 'bianca lopez'] --- H. G. Wellsâs âThe Croquet Playerâ Plays at East Village Theater Playwright Kris Kouros has transformed a little-known novella by Wells for the stage at Theater for the New City, an Off-Broadway venue known for its edgy and avant-garde productions. But hurry if you plan to see it: the last performance is set for April 4. There is something inherently alluring about the unpredictability of a show at Theater for the New City. Bold, gritty, and daring theater sets it apart from the streamlined âsafeâ choices dominating commercial theater. Mystery, splendor, and illusion await you in H. G. Wellsâs The Croquet Player, now playing at Theater for the New City. As you enter the theater, a soft brush against your skin briefly catches your attention, before you realize it is the resident feline of Theater for the New City, who roams the halls with quiet authority and a certain regal indifference. Making your way to your seat, you are met by the productionâs set design, the Perona Springs Hotel croquet yard, set in Normandy, France. A wrought iron garden table, 1940s-era elegance, croquet mallets, and stately columns evoke a setting that feels familiar to high society, but what unfolds is a mystery in itself. Kris Kouros adapts this little-known novella for the stage with a clear sensitivity to Wellsâs tone. The adaptation captures the obscurity of the events that unfold, along with a steady command of the dialogueâs cadence and a precise use of vernacular that feels both specific and resonant. Even language shaped in another era carries a surprising immediacy for contemporary audiences. Without a sense of purpose or direction, Georgie Frobisher (Daniel Yaiullo), a 33-year-old unmarried man who has never worked, remains dependent on his wealthy aunt, Miss Frobisher, and is clearly her favored companion. With a taste for fine menswear and refined habits, he addresses the audience in a manner that signals what is to come: a ghost story unlike any other. Georgie functions as an unlikely narrator, foreshadowing a narrative that blends surrealism and the occult with the very real anxieties of its historical moment, including the looming threat of Hitlerâs invasions during World War II. Fact and fiction begin to blur as the production unfolds. The stage itself becomes active, as smoke fills the space in waves, diffusing through the lighting. In these moments, the question emerges, is this a dreamlike sequence, or has the imagination taken hold? âIf we do not obey the rules, what becomes of civilization?â Miss Frobisher, played by the formidable Christine Weiss, exclaims to Georgie as they play croquet and discuss logistics. This line alone foreshadows the events of the play that follow, what becomes of mankind once rules of civility are broken and great fascist tyrants get hold of society? From her grief of seeing growing seagrass on the croquet yard to debating on the very difficult task of how to spend more of her exorbitant amount of wealth, even in her Madame de Pompadour embellishments, she is a much welcomed comedic relief throughout. At one point, Georgie hits the croquet ball way past the audience, where Miss Frobisher unenthusiastically has to chase after it, disappearing off stage with her boisterous voice resonating through the seats as she sighs in discomfort. Seeing her struggle, even just a tad, is a welcomed delight. Director Joe John Battista stages the actors in a way that no one particular character ever takes over entirely. There is a freedom he allows in letting actors have free rein over the entirety of the theater. Whether they appear from upstage, center stage, or downstage, we remain keen on wondering where actors will appear from next. Though the set never changes, a clever use of a projection screen and a silhouette screen helps to amplify intense moments in the play for dramatic effect. At one point, there is an eerie sight of a masked person appearing as death itself in between the foyer of the hotel hall that startles and never ceases to unsettle. The most powerful impact is the sound that blares above our heads when it comes to World War II sirens and announcements. Interactions between Georgie and a French waiter (John Barilla) lighten the complexity of the themes of the play. In certain ways, the caricature of the French waiter, with his thick clichÃ©d accent, overly apologetic demeanor, and fumbling back and forth, creates a hyper surrealism where, just when you think you have a grasp of this world, the looming events of active war taking place bring you right back to reality. Apart from excruciating yet comical bourgeois platitudes, a particularly comical moment is when the French waiter makes the ghastly mistake of serving milk versus cream, to which narrator Georgie doth protest. Georgieâs peculiar and highbrow requests make him the perfect specimen for being subjected to humility when the sword of Damocles dangles over his head in what is to come. Georgie is abashedly smitten with the dashingly handsome hotel guest and good doctor Finchatten. Brian Vincent is the actor who plays Finchatten, and the manner in which he physically stands and gestures shows a reassuring kindness that conveys a humble yet regal gentleman. Finchatten regales Georgie with tales of the oppressive rural area of Cainmarsh, where a mind virus is set to plague the people. From killing significant others to bashing in oneâs own beloved pet, madness runs rampant. A complete world away from the comfy luxuries of Perona Springs. Very much relevant to todayâs fears of catastrophic war, apart from shifts in attire and language, not much has changed. The Croquet Player remains eerily resonant in its portrayal of looming doom, masked by the comforts of everyday distraction. The anxieties that haunt us today, from recent pandemics to global conflict and societal unrest, mirror Wellsâs allegorical and introspective vision. This production succeeds in its ambitious telling of a world where, beneath the surface of wealth and civility, everything remains fragile in the face of crisis.

Art Center Workshop Fest author: The ; New York; Neighbor url: https://patch.com/new-york/upper-east-side-nyc/calendar/event/20260407/37fd3e7b-3002-44d0-add6-dc9c2f1274b2/free-art-center-workshop-fest hostname: patch.com description: Check out the latest community post from one of your neighbors. (The views expressed in this post are the author’s own.) sitename: Upper East Side, NY Patch date: 2026-04-01 categories: ['Calendar'] --- Sign up for a free 45-minute introductory class! Choose between jewelry making, fine art, or attending a ceramics demo drop in. Perfect for beginners and no experience required! Drawing Thinking about taking a drawing class but not sure where to start? Get a taste of the fundamentals, including line, shading, and composition. All supplies are provided — just show up and start drawing! If you enjoy this session, continue building your skills in one of our full-length drawing courses! Watercolor Always wanted to try watercolor? In just 45 minutes, you’ll learn basic techniques like washes, blending, and layering. With all supplies provided, you can simply experiment and see if watercolor is for you! If you love it, join one of our multi-week watercolor classes. Screen Printing Explore the art of screen printing — a process that involves pressing ink through a stenciled mesh screen to create bold, graphic designs — and learn how this technique evolved from practical use to a cornerstone of American art and culture. You will use pre-designed screens, set up a basic screen printing station, practice pulling prints, and create your own custom design on a canvas tote bag to take home. If you enjoy it, join us for our multi-week screen printing and printmaking classes! Ceramics Demo Experience the art of the potter’s wheel in this live ceramics throwing demonstration. Skilled instructors will transform clay into beautiful, functional forms right before your eyes. Get an inspiring glimpse into the craft, perfect for anyone curious about working with clay and interested in our multi-week handbuilding and wheel ceramic classes. Jewelry Pendant Create your own pendant in this hands-on jewelry workshop, perfect for all skill levels. Learn fundamental techniques as you shape, assemble, and finish a unique piece to take home. If you enjoy it, consider signing up for our multi-week Jewelry and Metalsmithing classes offered at all skill levels. During Workshop Fest, you’ll meet our talented instructors, get a hands-on feel for our programs, and discover your next artistic passion! Whether you’re looking to try something new or reconnect with your creative side, this is the perfect opportunity to get started. Spaces are limited, so be sure to reserve your spot! If you enjoy your experience, sign up for a class this spring. Our next session of art classes starts the week of April 13. Preview classes.

Research from ACT demonstrates that academic achievement by 8th Grade has a larger impact on college and career readiness than high school performance—underscoring why our N-8 model matters. In our youngest years, we foster social-emotional development and executive function skills during the period most critical for brain development. Elementary students build mastery in core academics while developing the critical thinking abilities that prepare them for increasingly complex work ahead. Through Middle School, faculty collaborate closely to design differentiated instruction that challenges every child appropriately. Research-based pedagogies help students master problem-solving and effective communication across all subjects. What distinguishes Rodeph Sholom School is our commitment to the whole child. Our mission extends beyond high test scores: we develop each student's love of learning and sense of responsibility to themselves and others. Through meaningful experiences—from buddy programs pairing older and younger students to extended studies across disciplines—children discover their capabilities while building lasting connections. articles.article_text: --- title: Drug Mart - Upper East Side, NY Patch url: https://patch.com/new-york/upper-east-side-nyc/business/listing/528496/drug-mart hostname: patch.com description: Find the Right Medication the First Time: Personalized Testing at Drug Mart Pharmacy End the Guesswork Have you ever taken a new medication only to... sitename: Upper East Side, NY Patch date: 2025-07-18 categories: ['Business Listing'] --- About Us Find the Right Medication the First Time: Personalized Testing at Drug Mart Pharmacy End the Guesswork Have you ever taken a new medication only to first worse? Or watched your child struggle with side effects? Or felt like nothing ever really helps? At Drug Mart Pharmacy, located at 1631 York Avenue (at 86th Street), New York, we understand how discouraging and confusing it can be to cycle through medications without answers. That’s why we now offer pharmacogenomic (PGx) testing—a revolutionary service that helps match you to the right medication before you even start taking it. This isn’t trial and error. It’s science, personalized. What Is Pharmacogenomic Testing? Pharmacogenomic testing looks at how your genes affect your body’s response to medications. Through a painless cheek swab, we can help identify: - Which medications are most likely to work for you - Which drugs could cause unpleasant or serious side effects - What dosages are appropriate for your body - What medications might not work at all This is precision medicine—tailored to you—and it’s available through your trusted community pharmacy. Why “One-Size-Fits-All” Doesn’t Work For decades, medications have been prescribed based on broad guidelines and population averages. But every body is different. Without knowing how your genes metabolize medications, you may experience: - Side effects like nausea, fatigue, or insomnia - No benefit at all from the drug - Dangerous interactions if you’re on multiple medications - Stress and frustration as you try new treatments over and over again At Drug Mart Pharmacy, we believe you deserve better. Pharmacogenomic testing puts you at the center of your treatment plan, offering clarity and control in place of confusion. NEW Community Endorsements Community Endorsements NEW
